ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a hummus entry (FOODON_03311737) but no specific entry for hummus topped with lamb. The hummus entry is used as the closest defensible anchor; the lamb component is documented in classification.main_ingredients.
ontology.foodex2_description: Hummus (chickpea paste/dip) — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for the hummus base; lamb topping is a composite addition not separately coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for the composite dish hummus-with-lamb. A0CKP (hummus) is used as the primary anchor. Lamb component is noted in main_ingredients.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hummus with lamb as a composite dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food composition codes for this dish type.

serving.description: 1 plate with hummus, lamb, and pita bread
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous spread of hummus topped with seasoned lamb pieces, accompanied by pita bread, as typically served in a Turkish or Middle Eastern restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Hummus, commercial (FDC ID 172453) and Lamb, ground, cooked, pan-browned (FDC ID 174382); composite estimate weighted approximately 55% hummus base and 45% cooked lamb by mass.
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for the composite dish hummus-with-lamb. Values are estimated by blending USDA data for commercial hummus (FDC ID 172453, ~55% weight contribution) and cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174382, ~45% weight contribution), consistent with a typical Turkish hummus-with-lamb preparation. Input per_100g values (energy 187.5 kcal, protein 11.25 g, fat 11.25 g, carbohydrate 11.25 g) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with minor rounding. Sodium 225 mg/100g is plausible for a seasoned preparation. All values are medium-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Chickpeas (hummus base) GI ~28; lamb contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not raise GI.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish hummus-with-lamb. The GI of 28 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for chickpeas/hummus (GI ~28, low category), which forms the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Lamb contributes protein and fat but negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (28) × available carbohydrate per serving (45 g from 400 g × 11.25 g/100g) / 100 = 12.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 225 mg/100g; estimated 900 mg per full 400 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit pita bread, as the full 400 g serving delivers approximately 900 mg sodium — 39% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — though the estimated potassium content (~280 mg/100g from chickpeas, tahini, and lamb) partially offsets the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~28) from chickpea base; 11.25 g carbohydrate and 2.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hummus with lamb is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index (~28) driven by chickpeas, high protein content (11.25 g/100g) that slows gastric emptying, and meaningful dietary fibre (2.5 g/100g); however, pita bread should be limited or substituted with wholemeal pita to avoid a refined-carbohydrate spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.75 g/100g from lamb; 15 g saturated fat per full 400 g serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size, as the full 400 g serving provides approximately 15 g saturated fat from lamb — 75% of the 20 g daily limit — while the olive oil and tahini in the hummus base contribute heart-healthy monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats that partially offset this concern; substituting lamb with grilled chicken or fish would meaningfully reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways including TNF-alpha and IL-6
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as prepared due to the lamb component; requesting a lamb-free version with extra chickpeas or substituting with grilled fish would make the dish RA-suitable, as the hummus base (olive oil, garlic, chickpeas) is anti-inflammatory.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hummus with Lamb (also known as Hummus Lamb or Hummus bil Lahm) is a Turkish and broader Middle Eastern dish consisting of a generous base of creamy hummus — made from blended chickpeas, tahini, olive oil, lemon juice, and garlic — topped with seasoned, tender lamb that has been pan-fried, grilled, or slow-cooked and then diced or shredded. The dish is typically served with pita bread and garnished with paprika, cumin, and a drizzle of olive oil. The texture contrasts the smooth, velvety hummus with the slightly chewy, richly spiced lamb. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 187.5 kcal, 11.25 g protein, 11.25 g fat (3.75 g saturated), and 11.25 g carbohydrate with 2.5 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is low (~28) due to the chickpea base, making it broadly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when pita bread is limited. Sodium at 225 mg/100g warrants attention for hypertension management at full serving sizes. The lamb component raises concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its arachidonic acid content. The dish is halal-certified in most preparations and is commonly found in Turkish restaurants and Middle Eastern eateries across Singapore and Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for the composite dish hummus-with-lamb. Nutrient values are estimated by blending USDA FoodData Central data for commercial hummus (FDC ID 172453) and cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174382) at an approximate 55:45 weight ratio. The input values from the GrabFood record are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database chickpea/hummus entry. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for commercial hummus (FDC ID 172453) and cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174382); no single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8) are estimated from the USDA composite and are approximate. 3. potassium_mg (280.0) is an estimate from chickpeas, tahini, and lamb components per USDA FoodData Central; not directly measured for this dish. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Turkish hummus-with-lamb preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; individual component data exist but reliable composite values cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. 5. The dish is listed under Turkish cuisine in the input; multilingual names for Singaporean mandatory languages (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) have been provided given the dish is sold in Singapore (country_of_origin: Singapore) and the record originates from a Singapore food delivery platform. 6. Pita bread is included in the standard 400 g serving and contributes refined carbohydrates and additional sodium; this is noted in health context guidance. 7. The GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on the chickpea/hummus GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for hummus-with-lamb was found in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024.
